fields.description:
## Why

The compaction fold claims an all-or-none contract (invariant I1): snapshot written
first, sources renamed to `*.retired`, and on failure every completed rename reversed
and the snapshot removed. But in `src/rebar/_commands/compact.py` (~lines 235-247) the
rollback path only *logs* a failed reverse-rename and continues, then unconditionally
removes the snapshot and returns failure. Because normal replay excludes `*.retired`
(`src/rebar/reducer/_cache.py`), a source that failed to restore becomes invisible with
no snapshot left to carry its folded effect — silent, undetectable data loss that
violates the stated contract.

## What

Chosen approach (committed — not "retain OR sentinel"): **retain the snapshot.** The
snapshot already carries the folded effect of every source, so keeping it is the minimal
change and needs no new on-disk artifact. On any reverse-rename failure during rollback,
skip the `os.remove(snapshot)` step, emit an explicit `rollback incomplete; run fsck`
diagnostic, and return failure.

Key realization from the fsck code: with the snapshot retained, the residual mixed state
is **already detectable and repairable by existing fsck** — no new issue kind is needed:
- A source that WAS reversed back to an active `.json` is a UUID in the snapshot's
  `source_event_uuids` whose active file "still exists" → the existing
  `SNAPSHOT_INCONSISTENT` check (`src/rebar/_commands/fsck.py:302`) fires, and it is
  already in the `--repair-snapshots` rebuild trigger (`fsck.py:191`).
- A source that failed to reverse stays `*.retired` and folded in the snapshot — i.e. the
  normal healthy compacted state; no false positive (crucially, this means the detection
  does NOT fire on healthy fully-compacted tickets).
So this story's only production change is: **do not delete the snapshot** in the
rollback-failure branch (plus the diagnostic). The existing `SNAPSHOT_INCONSISTENT` +
`--repair-snapshots` path already reconciles the mixed state (confirmed against
`fsck.py`), so **no fsck code change is expected**.

Two correctness facts, confirmed against the code (correcting earlier drafts):
- **Repair mechanism is REBUILD, not retire-only.** `--repair-snapshots` routes a
  `SNAPSHOT_INCONSISTENT` finding to `rebuild_snapshot_from_full_log`
  (`src/rebar/_commands/fsck.py:193-195`, `compact.py:289`), which WRITES A NEW SNAPSHOT
  from the full ordered log and retires the live raws. So the repair test must assert a
  new `*-SNAPSHOT.json` IS written and the formerly-active source IS renamed to
  `*.retired`, and a follow-up `fsck` exits 0 — NOT "no new snapshot written."
- **Reads are already safe in the mixed window (no double-count).** The reversed-to-active
  source keeps its ORIGINAL (old) filename, so it sorts BEFORE the retained snapshot;
  normal replay short-circuits on the snapshot and skips all pre-snapshot events
  (`src/rebar/reducer/_api.py`, `_cache.py:110-113` positional skip), so the folded event
  is not re-applied. The mixed state is therefore read-correct BEFORE repair; the
  `rollback incomplete` diagnostic tells the operator to run `fsck --repair-snapshots` to
  NORMALIZE the hygiene inconsistency (`SNAPSHOT_INCONSISTENT`), not because reads are
  wrong.
- **Durability of the decision.** Add an architectural comment at the retained-snapshot
  branch in `compact.py` explaining WHY the snapshot is intentionally not removed (so a
  future refactor doesn't "fix" it back into the data-loss hazard), and note the
  compact→fsck recovery contract in the concurrency docs.

- Add fault-injection tests (currently the negative suite injects only at
  `compact.lock.acquire` and `compact._git`, never in the rename loop).

## Scope

- `src/rebar/_commands/compact.py` (rollback-failure branch: retain snapshot + diagnostic)
- `src/rebar/_commands/fsck.py` (verify the existing `_check_snapshot`/`_scan` +
  `--repair-snapshots` rebuild covers the mixed state; extend only if a gap is found)
- `tests/interfaces/lifecycle/test_compact_negative.py` (new fault-injection tests)

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Forward-rename fault test: inject `OSError` on the 2nd/3rd forward `os.rename`;
      assert nonzero exit, no active snapshot, every original filename restored, no
      stray `*.retired`, reduced state deep-equal to pre-compaction, clean `fsck`, and
      an idempotent re-compaction.
- [ ] Reverse-rename fault test: inject a forward failure AND a reverse-rename failure
      (so at least one source is reversed-to-active and at least one stays `*.retired`);
      assert the snapshot is NOT deleted, a recoverable diagnostic is emitted, and no
      source data is lost.
- [ ] In the mixed state BEFORE repair, a read is correct: the reducer's event-UUID set +
      payloads deep-equal the pre-compaction reduced state (the snapshot's positional skip
      excludes the reversed-to-active source; no double-count). The `rollback incomplete`
      diagnostic directs the operator to run `fsck --repair-snapshots`.
- [ ] `fsck` (no flag) reports `SNAPSHOT_INCONSISTENT` for the reversed-to-active
      source in that mixed state (existing detection), and does NOT flag a healthy
      fully-compacted ticket.
- [ ] `fsck --repair-snapshots` reconciles by REBUILDING via
      `rebuild_snapshot_from_full_log`: assert a NEW `*-SNAPSHOT.json` is written, the
      formerly-active source is renamed to `*.retired`, a follow-up `fsck` exits 0, and
      the reduced state is unchanged (deep-equal to pre-repair).
- [ ] An architectural comment in `compact.py` documents why the snapshot is retained on
      rollback failure, and the compact→fsck recovery contract is noted in
      `docs/concurrency.md`.
- [ ] `make lint && make typecheck && make test` pass.
